topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Sequences and Series > Recurrence relations
Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Sequences and Series > Sums and products
prompt: Let $a_0, a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n, \dots$ be a sequence of numbers satisfying $(3 - a_{n+1}) \cdot (6 + a_n) = 18$, and $a_0 = 3$. Then $\sum_{i=0}^{n} \frac{1}{a_i}$ equals ________.
response_text:
From  
\[
(3-a_{n+1})(6+a_n)=18
\]
we solve for \(a_{n+1}\):
\[
3-a_{n+1}=\frac{18}{6+a_n}
\]
\[
a_{n+1}=3-\frac{18}{6+a_n}=\frac{3a_n}{6+a_n}.
\]

Now invert:
\[
\frac1{a_{n+1}}=\frac{6+a_n}{3a_n}=\frac{2}{a_n}+\frac13.
\]
So
\[
\frac1{a_{n+1}}-\frac1{a_n}=\frac1{a_n}+\frac13,
\]
but a cleaner way is to set \(b_n=\frac1{a_n}\). Then
\[
b_{n+1}=\frac{2}{3}b_n+\frac13.
\]
Since \(a_0=3\), we have \(b_0=\frac13\). This gives
\[
b_{n+1}-1=\frac23(b_n-1),
\]
so
\[
b_n=1-\frac23\left(\frac23\right)^n = 1-\left(\frac23\right)^{n+1}.
\]
Hence
\[
\frac1{a_n}=1-\left(\frac23\right)^{n+1}.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{i=0}^n \frac1{a_i}
=\sum_{i=0}^n \left(1-\left(\frac23\right)^{i+1}\right)
=(n+1)-\sum_{i=0}^n \left(\frac23\right)^{i+1}.
\]
The geometric sum is
\[
\sum_{i=0}^n \left(\frac23\right)^{i+1}
=\frac23\cdot \frac{1-(2/3)^{n+1}}{1-2/3}
=2\left(1-\left(\frac23\right)^{n+1}\right).
\]
So
\[
\sum_{i=0}^n \frac1{a_i}
=(n+1)-2+2\left(\frac23\right)^{n+1}
= n-1+2\left(\frac23\right)^{n+1}.
\]

Final answer: \(\boxed{n-1+2\left(\frac23\right)^{n+1}}\)
